methodology: Structural lifts are slow, longer-arc gains that compound across generations: vaccine coverage, electrification, internet access, life expectancy, renewable energy share. Sources are Our World in Data, World Bank, IEA, ITU, WHO, and UNESCO.
data_status: illustrative — pipeline not yet live; values approximate published figures

panels.title: Global under-5 mortality rate
panels.subtitle: Deaths per 1,000 live births
panels.source: UN IGME / Our World in Data
panels.framing: Roughly 7 million fewer children under five die each year compared to 1990. Driven by vaccines, oral rehydration therapy, skilled birth attendance, and rising household incomes.

panels.title: Global renewable share of electricity generation
panels.subtitle: Hydro + wind + solar + bioenergy as % of generation
panels.framing: The crossover that mattered: in 2023, more new generating capacity added globally was renewable than fossil. Solar and wind costs have fallen ~90% and ~70% respectively over the period.

panels.title: Global internet usage
panels.subtitle: Percent of population online
panels.framing: From under 7% to over 70% in 24 years. Mobile-first access has been the lever in the developing world. Quality and freedom of access are not measured here.

panels.title: DTP3 immunization coverage of one-year-olds
panels.subtitle: Global average, all infants
panels.source: WHO/UNICEF Estimates of National Immunization Coverage
panels.framing: From a quarter of infants covered in 1980 to over four-fifths now. Pandemic disruption visible in the 2021 dip; recovery underway. DTP3 is the standard benchmark for system performance.
